course_title: Special Topics:Literature: Witches and Wise Women
subject: ENG
course_description: Literary topics vary. May be repeated for credit when topic varies.
course_notes: (Complete Topic - Witches and Wise Women: Early Modern English Texts and Their Contemporary Retellings) (How do early modern English plays about witches and wise women shape contemporary narratives about race, gender, sexuality, and ability? In this course, we'll explore both early modern English drama and recent retellings, examining plots about uncanny figures who variously wield magical power, voice sexual desire, embody gender variance and racial difference, and navigate marginalization. We'll analyze Shakespeare's Macbeth and The Tempest, focusing on the Weird Sisters and Sycorax, and we'll explore a trio of witch plays by Shakespeare's peers in which fear of female power is writ large. We'll also engage with modern appropriations of these works from long ago, such as Nalo Hopkinson's "Shift" and Gloria Naylor's Mama Day (revisitings of The Tempest in a Caribbean-inspired setting and a fictional island off the Georgia coast), Jo Nesbø's Macbeth (noir crime meets prophecy), Maggie O'Farrell's Hamnet (about parental grief as well as female herbal and intuitive knowledge), and Jeanette Winterson's The Daylight Gate (about the 1612 Lancashire witch trials). And we will watch Robert Eggers' acclaimed film The Witch (2015) about female sexuality and social control. Over the semester, you'll have many opportunities to practice close reading, conduct historical research, lead and take part in wide-ranging discussions, and design a project connecting what we read to contemporary narratives about women. Fulfills "English Lit Before 1700' and "Diverse Perspectives 2" Reqs.
